quotes.text: She doesn't need you to save her. She needs you to get out of her way.
quotes.speaker: Aliens (1986)
quotes.context: Ripley dressing down a colonial marine who underestimates her
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Ripley's no-nonsense authority and the military setting make this feel right at home, and the rhythm matches Weaver's clipped delivery in that film. The giveaway is it's a hair too evenly constructed; Ripley's real put-downs tend to be more situational and messier.

quotes.text: You want the money, you take the room. That's always been the deal.
quotes.speaker: Chinatown (1974)
quotes.context: Jake Gittes pressing a shady landlord for information about a tenant
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Gittes talks in clipped, transactional half-threats that fit the noir register perfectly, and 'that's always been the deal' sounds like the kind of worn-out corruption-speak the film wallows in. The subtle giveaway is that Gittes is more sardonic than blunt; he'd usually twist the knife with a little more irony.

quotes.text: Seventeen years on the same corner and I never once saw a cop buy his own coffee.
quotes.speaker: The Departed (2006)
quotes.context: A South Boston neighborhood fixture making a dry aside to Costigan
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Scorsese's Boston dialogue is full of this kind of sideways social observation delivered like it's nothing, and the detail about free coffee grounds it in working-class texture. The giveaway is it's slightly too aphoristic; the film's real throwaway lines are even rougher and less neatly framed.

quotes.text: Nobody writes letters anymore. That's why I trust them.
quotes.speaker: The Talented Mr. Ripley (1999)
quotes.context: Tom Ripley explaining his forgery habit to Meredith
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Ripley's self-satisfied little paradoxes and Minghella's sun-drenched European dialogue make this feel at home in that film's register. The giveaway is it's a fraction too clever and self-aware; the film's Tom tends to expose himself accidentally rather than through deliberate wit.
